coredata.dc:title: Lubrication behavior of fluorescent graphene quantum dots hybrid polyethylene glycol lubricant
coredata.dc:creator: Zeng, Zihan
Li, Wen
Li, Yuting
Zhao, Zhuang
Lin, Kaiyue
Fan, Xiaoqiang
Zhu, Minhao
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with a lattice structure and the size of 1.8–3.4 nm were prepared by a simple hydrogen peroxide-containing hydrothermal reaction method using graphene oxide (GO) as a precursor. The hydroxyl group with the cutting effect can cut GO into GQDs with a large number of oxygen-containing functional groups. As polyethylene glycol (PEG) additives, the photoluminescence (PL) properties of well-dispersed GQDs can reflect and monitor the lubrication state between the sliding interfaces because partial GQDs contributed to low friction/wear and occurred the structural transformation. As-modulated hybrid PEG with 0.04 wt% GQDs shows excellent wear reduction ability, and the wear rate is reduced by 40.5%. The formation of a dense disordered carbon film on the sliding surfaces and iron/iron oxide debris with attached GQDs between sliding interfaces contribute to filling the pits and hindering the direct contact of friction pairs, thereby enhancing the wear resistance. This work illustrates that GQDs as a polar lubricant additive have a great application potential in lubrication regulation and monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Polyethylene glycol
Photoluminescence
Lubricant additive
Tribological property

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of GQDs and GQDs hybrid PEG dispersion
Characterizations of GO, GQDs and GQDs hybrid PEG dispersion
Tribological properties of GQDs as a PEG lubricant additive
Analyses of wear surfaces by SEM, EDS, and Raman characterizations
Analyses of worn debris by SEM, EDS, and TEM characterizations

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support provided by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52075458 and U2141211), and Analytical and Testing Center of Southwest Jiaotong University for providing testing services.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) SEM diagram of GO. Distributions of (b) C and (c) O elements of GO. (d) AFM image and (e) thickness of GO. (f) Raman spectra of GO and GQDs.
(a), (b) TEM images of GQDs. (c) Image of lubricants with different GQDs concentrations. XPS spectra of (d) GQDs, (e) C1s and (f) O1s.

(a) UV–Vis absorption, PLE and PL spectra of GQDs at room temperature. (b) PL spectra of GQDs at an excitation wavelength of 320 nm-420 nm. PL spectra of GQDs at an excitation wavelength of 280 nm-380 nm (c) before and (d) after friction.
(a) Variation of friction coefficients with time and (b) mean friction coefficients under 50 N. (c) Variation of friction coefficients with time and (d) mean friction coefficients under 392 N.

(a) Wear rates under 50 N. (b) Worn scar diameters under 392 N. 3D images of worn tracks and section curves under (c) GQDs-0, (d) GQDs-0.04, (e) GQDs-0.08 and (f) GQDs-0.12. 3D images of worn scars and section curves under (g) GQDs-0, (h) GQDs-0.04, (i) GQDs-0.08 and (j) GQDs-0.12.

Analyses of surfaces of worn tracks under 50 N: GQDs-0: (a), (b) SEM images, distribution of (c) C element, (d) O element and (e) spectrum of EDS mapping; GQDs-0.04: (f), (g) SEM images, distribution of (h) C element, (i) O element and (j) spectrum of EDS mapping; GQDs-0.08: (k), (l) SEM images, distribution of (m) C element, (n) O element and (o) spectrum of EDS mapping; GQDs-0.12: (p), (q) SEM images, distribution of (r) C element, (s) O element and (t) spectrum of EDS mapping.

Analyses of surfaces of worn scars under 392 N: GQDs-0: (a), (b) SEM images, distribution of (c) C element, (d) O element and (e) spectrum of EDS mapping; GQDs-0.04: (f), (g) SEM images, distribution of (h) C element, (i) O element and (j) spectrum of EDS mapping; GQDs-0.08: (k), (l) SEM images, distribution of (m) C element, (n) O element and (o) spectrum of EDS mapping; GQDs-0.12: (p), (q) SEM images, distribution of (r) C element, (s) O element and (t) spectrum of EDS mapping.
Raman analyses of (a) worn tracks under 50 N and (b) worn scars under 392 N. (c) The image, (d) Raman mapping and (e) Raman analyses of worn tracks of GQDs-0.04.

Under 50 N load, (a), (b) SEM and (c) EDS mapping spectrum of worn debris, distribution diagrams of (d) C element, (e) O element and (f) Fe element. (g) TEM of worn debris. (h), (i) HRTEM of GQDs on worn debris.
Mechanism diagram of GQDs forming friction film.
Schematic diagram of GQDs preparation process.

head.ce:title: Lubrication behavior of fluorescent graphene quantum dots hybrid polyethylene glycol lubricant

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Advanced Technologies of Materials (Ministry of Education), School of Materials Science and Engineering, Southwest Jiaotong University, Chengdu 210031, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Advanced Technologies of Materials (Ministry of Education)
School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Advanced Technologies of Materials (Ministry of Education), School of Materials Science and Engineering, Southwest Jiaotong University, Chengdu 210031, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Tribology Research Institute, School of Mechanical Engineering, Southwest Jiaotong University, Chengdu 610031, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Tribology Research Institute, School of Mechanical Engineering, Southwest Jiaotong University, Chengdu 610031, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with a lattice structure and the size of 1.8–3.4 nm were prepared by a simple hydrogen peroxide-containing hydrothermal reaction method using graphene oxide (GO) as a precursor. The hydroxyl group with the cutting effect can cut GO into GQDs with a large number of oxygen-containing functional groups. As polyethylene glycol (PEG) additives, the photoluminescence (PL) properties of well-dispersed GQDs can reflect and monitor the lubrication state between the sliding interfaces because partial GQDs contributed to low friction/wear and occurred the structural transformation. As-modulated hybrid PEG with 0.04 wt% GQDs shows excellent wear reduction ability, and the wear rate is reduced by 40.5%. The formation of a dense disordered carbon film on the sliding surfaces and iron/iron oxide debris with attached GQDs between sliding interfaces contribute to filling the pits and hindering the direct contact of friction pairs, thereby enhancing the wear resistance. This work illustrates that GQDs as a polar lubricant additive have a great application potential in lubrication regulation and monitoring.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors are unable or have chosen not to specify which data has been used.

ce:section.ce:para: Friction and wear can inevitably occur in the operating machine, the wear caused by friction is the main cause of mechanical equipment failure. About 80% of the damaged parts are caused by various forms of wear, which greatly increases the cost of operating machine [1–5]. Therefore, the pursuit of efficient and environmentally sustainable lubricating oil is one of the most effective methods to reduce energy loss and achieve low carbon emission [6,7]. Synthetic oils have good lubricity, high viscosity index, oxidation stability at high temperature or other properties required by practical lubricants, but their application range is limited due to high cost and poor friction properties [8]. As an excellent water-based lubricant, polyethylene glycol (PEG) is widely used in high-performance oils, gear oils and compressor lubricating oils for its environmental degradability. Most of the chemical properties of PEG are similar and often need to combine with additives to impart their specific properties, especially anti-friction and anti-wear additives [9]. Traditional additives usually contain elements such as P and S, the performance of these additives has reached the limit. Nanoparticles are considered to be the most feasible and effective additives to improve system efficiency, reduce energy loss, lower the emission of harmful compounds and weaken the impact on the environment [10–13].

It becomes a research hotspot that nanoparticles are used as additives to prepare lubricants with low friction and wear. Gupta et al. [14] dispersed graphene nanosheets in PEG to form chemical grafting. Compared with no additives, the friction coefficient and wear were reduced by 26% and 32% respectively. Saini et al. [15] used polytetrafluoroethylene nanoparticles as extreme pressure additives, which could not only improve the extreme pressure performance of selected oils (up to 345%) but also reduce friction (∼35%) and wear (∼35%). Ghaednia et al. [16] directly prepared silver nanoparticles in liquid PEG, up to 35% reduction of the friction coefficient and wear. The mechanism of reducing friction and wear of nanoparticles can be divided into the following points: (a) Turning the sliding friction into a combination of rolling and sliding friction [17,18]; (b) Preventing direct contact of friction surfaces by producing films [19–21]; (c) Filling the pits by dynamic deposition and self-healing [22]; (d) Playing a polishing effect and flattening the worn surface [23]. However, the dispersion of nanoparticles in the lubricating medium is a serious problem due to the different cohesion between each other. When the agglomeration of nanoparticles leads to the reduction of the shear effect, it will lead to problems such as clogging and contact starvation [24–26].

Graphene has a high surface area and excellent charge transfer properties, moreover graphene with a layered structure could be well-dispersed in the lubricants, it can reduce friction by weakening the shear force [27]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have a zero-dimensional carbon nanostructure with a single or few layers of graphene, mainly distribute in the range of 3–20 nm. GQDs show unique optical, electronic and physicochemical properties due to the quantum confinement effects and edge effects, such as photoluminescence (PL) and electroluminescence, redox, interfacial activity, etc [28]. Generally, the origin of PL of GQDs can be attributed to two kinds: intrinsic state emission related to size, edge type and recombination of localized electron-hole pairs, and defect state emission related to defects in GQDs [29,30]. Special performance of GQDs is given by certain synthesis methods, GQDs with excellent polarity have good dispersion can be evenly dispersed in PEG. Therefore, it is of great significance to explore the lubrication effect of graphene in quantum dimensions and reflect the lubricant state with its unique fluorescence effect.

In this paper, environmentally friendly additives GQDs were synthesized by a simple hydrothermal reaction and dispersed into PEG in low dosage. The tribological properties of lubricants with different concentrations were investigated under low load (50 N) and high load (392 N). Various characterization methods were used to explore the anti-wear mechanism of GQDs and prove the formation of deposited carbon film, the possibility of using the luminous intensity of fluorescent GQDs hybrid PEG lubricants to monitor the lubrication state and the service life was proposed.

Graphene oxide (GO) was obtained from Suzhou Tanfeng Graphene Technology Co. Ltd. (Suzhou, China). Ethanol, hydrogen peroxide and PEG were purchased from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Co. Ltd. (Chengdu, China). The whole experiment used deionized water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of GQDs and GQDs hybrid PEG dispersion
ce:section.ce:para: GQDs were prepared by simple hydrothermal reaction, filtration and freeze-drying as depicted in Scheme 1 . The precursor of this experiment was GO. GO (5 mg) with few layers was dispersed in ethanol (15 ml) for ultrasonic treatment for 2 h to obtain a gray-black suspension. Hydrogen peroxide (15 ml) was added and kept at 180 ℃ in a 100 ml stainless steel autoclave for 2 h to obtain yellow transparent liquid. The prepared solution was filtered by a 0.22 μm filter membrane and then subjected to rotary evaporation and freeze-drying to obtain colloidal GQDs. GQDs were used as an additive of PEG, and the lubricants were prepared as follows: colloidal GQDs were added into PEG to prepare lubricants with different concentrations of GQDs, for convenience of expression, lubricants were named as GQDs-0 (PEG without GQDs), GQDs-0.04 (the concentration of GQDs was 0.04 wt%), GQDs-0.08 (the concentration of GQDs was 0.08 wt%) and GQDs-0.12 (the concentration of GQDs was 0.12 wt%).

Atomic force microscopy (AFM, FM-Nanoview1000) was used to observe the morphology and lamellar thickness of GO, the model of AFM probe is Tap190Al with a radius of 10 nm, a force constant of 48 N/m and a resonance frequency of 190 kHz. The images of GQDs were observed by transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100F) with an acceleration voltage of 200 kV, the lattice characteristics of GQDs were measured by HRTEM to verify whether the prepared quantum dots were graphene. The composition of materials and the bonding state of related elements were confirmed by X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, ESCALAB 250Xi) with the excitation source of Al Kα rays. UV–Vis spectra (UV-2600, Shimadzu) were used to reflect the energy level transition of GQDs in the 200–600 nm spectral region. PL performance and photoluminescence excitation (PLE) performance were obtained by FLS980 (Edinburgh Instruments) spectrometer with a 450 W Xenon lamp. Raman spectrometer (Horiba labram HR800 Raman microscope) was used to analyze the prepared GQDs and GO, to observe the distribution characteristics of C characteristic peaks with an excitation wavelength of 532 nm. The kinematic viscosities of the lubricants were obtained by testing at 40 °C and 100 °C with the SYD-265 Kinematic Viscosity Tester.

In order to study the tribological properties of the prepared lubricants, the friction operating conditions were divided into two conditions in this experiment, and the application effects of lubricants under the two conditions were explored. Tribological experiments were conducted by a reciprocating friction machine (UMT-3) under low load (50 N) and a four-ball friction machine under high load (392 N), repeated experiments were carried out at least three times respectively. The upper ball and disc of the reciprocating friction machine were made of GCr15 steel (AISI 52100). The upper ball reciprocated with a displacement amplitude of 0.5 mm and pressure acted downward on the fixed disc. Four balls made of GCr15 steels were used in the four-ball friction machine under high load, with the upper ball rotating (1200 rpm) and pressure also acted downward on the fixed three balls. The 3D images of worn tracks and worn spots were recorded by white light interferometer (WLI, Contour GT, Germany), the morphology and elemental contents were analyzed by a JSM-IT500 scanning electron microscope (SEM) with a tungsten filament as an electron gun and an energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS). Raman spectra of the worn surfaces were measured at the excitation wavelength of 532 nm, and the carbon characteristic peaks were analyzed. The worn debris after friction were collected to analyze by SEM and EDS to observe morphologies and elements distributions on it, TEM was used to examine the morphologies of the wear debris and the lattice stripes of GQDs on wear debris.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations of GO, GQDs and GQDs hybrid PEG dispersion
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the accuracy of the precursor material, thickness and morphology of the GO were confirmed by SEM and AFM. Fig. 1 (a) shows that GO has a lamella structure and the wrinkled edges. The C and O elements densely distributed on GO as shown in Fig. 1(b) and Fig. 1(c) respectively. Fig. 1(d) and Fig. 1(e) are the AFM images of GO, indicating that the purchased GO has a stratified structure with a thickness of about 4 nm, which is in line with the characteristics of few layers of GO. Raman properties of graphene are affected by carbon atom mass, covalent bond length, external field and other factors [31]. Both materials of GO and GQDs have D and G peaks of C characteristic peaks as displayed in Raman analysis (Fig. 1(f)). D peak near 1340 cm−1 is usually considered as the disordered vibration peak of graphene. The specific position of this peak is related to the laser wavelength. It is caused by the lattice vibration leaving the center of Brillouin zone, which is used to characterize the structural defects or edges in graphene samples. G peak near 1580 cm−1 is the main characteristic peak of graphene, which is caused by the in-plane vibration of sp2 carbon atom. This peak can effectively reflect the number of layers of graphene, but it is very easy to be affected by stress. Overall, the Raman analyses portray that the GQDs are graphitic in nature [32].

As-prepared GQDs have monodispersity as shown in the TEM image of Fig. 2 , Fig. 2(a) portrays the statistic size distribution of GQDs ranges from 1.8 nm to 3.4 nm, with the majority being 2–3 nm. Fig. 2(b) displays the HRTEM image, indicating that the sample is crystalline with a spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the graphene (100) planes [33]. Fig. 2(c) provides optical images of lubricants, PEG without the additive shows transparent colorless, while after adding GQDs, the solutions become light yellow transparent, and the yellow is gradually obvious with the increase of GQDs concentrations. Fig. 2(c) indicates that GQDs have great dispersibility, corresponding to the small size of GQDs confirmed in Fig. 2(a). These features show that GQDs as an additive of PEG have an excellent research and application prospect. Fig. 2(d) reveals the XPS spectrum of GQDs, in which C1s appears at about 284.8 eV and O1s peak appears at about 532.4 eV. To further analyze the XPS measurement results, high-resolution XPS measurements were carried out. Fig. 2(e) shows the XPS spectrum of the C1s peak of GQDs, there are four peaks at 284.7, 286.1, 287.4 and 288.8 eV, corresponding to C–C/C = C in aromatic rings, C-O, C = O, and COOH bonds, respectively [34,35]. Fig. 2(f) depicts the XPS spectrum of the O1s peak of GQDs, there are three peaks at 530.9, 532.1 and 532.8 eV, corresponding to C = O, C-O and C-O-C/C-OH bonds, respectively [36,37].

To explore the fluorescence properties of GQDs, the analyses of UV–Vis absorption and PL properties were carried out. In the UV absorption spectrum (Fig. 3 (a)) of GQDs, there are two peaks at 266 nm and 360 nm, which are assigned to the p-p* transition of atomic sp2 domains and the n-p* transition of C = O bond in oxygen functional groups [38,39]. Fig. 3(a) provides the PL and PLE spectrum curves, the strongest emission at 463 nm (blue line) when excited by 404 nm (red line) wavelength. The PL mechanism of GQDs may originate from the high concentration of free zigzag sites [40]. As PLE spectrum curve had the strongest intensity at 404 nm, the PL spectra of GQDs under different excitation wavelengths (320–420 nm) were explored. Fig. 3(b) illustrates the PL behavior of GQDs is less affected by the excitation wavelength, showing excitation-independent PL behavior. It is different from general carbon-based materials, and have the strongest luminescence intensity under 400 nm excitation [41,42]. GQDs were dispersed into PEG to investigate the PL behavior of GQDs before-friction (Fig. 3(c)) and after-friction (Fig. 3(d)). The illustrations show the pictures under sunlight and UV irradiation of lubricants before-friction and after-friction, respectively. The fluorescence of lubricants after friction decreased slightly, probably due to partial GQDs being attached to friction pairs during friction process, thereby resulting in changes in the concentration of GQDs in the lubricants. Meanwhile a small amount of worn debris was produced, resulting in the deepening of yellow under sunlight. The PL behavior of GQDs is a size-dependent phenomenon, the shape and surface chemical properties of defects have also been found to have an impact. Dangling bonds and surface defects in GQDs may lead to carrier capture, inhibit radiation recombination and potentially quench emission. GQDs have potential applications in monitoring the condition of lubricants and exploring the service life of lubricants as the changes in the PL behavior of lubricants before and after friction, which is due to the change in the concentration of GQDs and the generation of Fe3+ by frictional chemical reactions.

Table. 1 lists the physical properties (density and viscosity index) of the lubricants used in the experiment. Due to the low content of GQDs, the densities of the lubricants remain, which has little effect on the possibility of causing metal corrosion. However, the viscosity indexes of the lubricants increase slightly with the increase of GQDs concentrations because GQDs are solid additives. The increase of viscosity index means that the lubricants are less affected by temperature and have better viscosity temperature performance. In this experiment, GQDs as an additive had little effect on the physical properties of PEG, PEG maintained excellent physical properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tribological properties of GQDs as a PEG lubricant additive
ce:section.ce:para: GQDs prepared by a hydrothermal method have good hydrophilicity, therefore GQDs can be evenly dispersed in PEG and well compatible with compatibility with polar lubricants. In this work, the tribological performances of different concentrations of GQDs as a PEG lubricant additive were investigated under low load and high load. As shown in Fig. 4 (a) and Fig. 4(b), the sliding friction experiments are implemented under low load testing condition (load: 50 N, reciprocating frequency: 5 Hz, time: 60 min, room temperature). When the concentration of GQDs increased from 0 to 0.12 wt%, the friction coefficients have no significant difference, adding GQDs has no great effect in reducing the friction coefficients. The friction experiments were carried out using the four-ball friction machine to analyze the friction coefficients under the high load condition (load: 392 N, rotating speed: 1200 rpm, time: 60 min, room temperature). The friction coefficients shown in Fig. 4(c) and Fig. 4(d) indicate that the addition of GQDs plays a little effect in reducing the friction coefficients.

Fig. 5 (a) expresses the wear volumes of different concentrations of GQDs under 50 N, the wear volumes are reduced up to 40.5% at GQDs-0.04, indicating that GQDs perform well in reducing wear volumes. Fig. 5((c)-(f)) display 3D images of worn tracks under different GQDs concentrations. Fig. 5(c) reveals that the surface lubricated by GQDs-0 is seriously worn, with deep furrows and obvious scratch lines along the friction direction, and the maximum depth of worn track is about 1.3 μm. The worn surfaces display different degrees of improvements and the depths of worn tracks decrease obviously after adding different concentrations of GQDs. Fig. 5(b) presents the worn scar diameters of different concentrations of GQDs under 392 N, the worn scar diameters decrease after adding GQDs and the minimum worn scar diameter is 0.0579 mm at GQDs-0.04, which demonstrates that GQDs have certain anti-wear performance under high load. At GQDs-0.08 (Fig. 5(e)), the shallowest worn track appears, and the depth is about 0.65 μm. Fig. 5((g)-(j)) show 3D images of steel balls surfaces at different GQDs addition concentrations. Fig. 5(g) illustrates that the surface lubricated by GQDs-0 is critically worn, with many deep furrows along the friction direction. The wear of some areas of the worn surfaces is improved and the furrows are shallower after adding GQDs. The above results manifest that the addition of GQDs contributes to improve the wear resistance of the lubricants, the wear resistances are improved more significantly under 50 N load than 392 N load. Under the load of 50 N, the minimum wear rate is obtained with GQDs-0.04. With the increase of GQDs concentration, the occurrence of adhesive wear induced by the aggregated GQDs between friction pairs [7].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analyses of wear surfaces by SEM, EDS, and Raman characterizations
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the wear reduction mechanism of GQDs hybrid PEG, the wear morphologies were analyzed. According to the characterization of worn tracks by SEM and EDS, the influence of GQDs on worn surface and anti-wear mechanism were further revealed. The characterization analyses under low load are shown in Fig. 6 . GQDs-0 has the most adhesive wear and deep furrows, the worn track width reaches 292 μm (Fig. 6(a)), and the least content of C element (Fig. 6(e)) appears. After adding GQDs, the widths of worn tracks decrease by 19–27 μm, the minimum worn track width appears at GQDs-0.04 (Fig. 6(f)). There are less wear and relatively flat surface at GQDs-0.08 (Fig. 6(k)), which is consistent with EDS analysis that the ratios of C/O element content (Fig. 6(o)) is the most at this concentration. The surfaces of the worn scars formed in the friction experiments under high load can be observed in Fig. 7 . Adhesive wear and abrasive wear were created in the friction process. The adhesive wear along the friction trajectories is arranged continuously under GQDs-0 (Fig. 7(a)). However, the adhesive wear and abrasive wear along with the friction direction decreased after adding GQDs. EDS analyses of the worn scars in Fig. 7 show that C and O elements are distributed along the friction trajectories. Additions of GQDs increase the C element on the worn surfaces as shown in Fig. 7, the C element is densely distributed in EDS images under the two friction conditions and increases after addition of GQDs, indicating that the GQDs participate in the friction reaction playing an important role in reducing wear. These analyses provide proofs for the formation of dense friction films between sliding interfaces.

To further research the experimental mechanism, Raman analyses of worn tracks and worn scars formed under two different friction conditions were characterized. Fig. 8 shows that after adding GQDs, there are two peaks about 1340 cm−1 and 1560 cm−1 corresponding to the D peak and G peak of C characteristic peak respectively. The result indicates that GQDs are involved in the reaction and deposited on the contact interfaces under the action of friction stress. Under 50 N load (Fig. 8(a)), D peak and G peak have similar intensities. Under 392 N load (Fig. 8(b)), the intensities of D peaks of GQDs-0.04 and GQDs-0.08 are higher than the G peaks, showing the deposited carbon with more disorderly. According to the front results, GQDs-0.04 has the highest wear reduction rate under 50 N. As the compactness of the friction film is essential to reduce wear, the tracks formed after friction of the GQDs-0.04 under 50 N were characterized by Raman mapping. As shown in Fig. 8(d), the red region is the area with the highest content of deposited carbon, and the deposited carbon contained gradually decreases in green and blue. A large area of unevenly distributed deposited carbon on the worn tracks, caused by the load distribution and roughness of the friction interfaces during the friction process [43]. Raman analyses of the three different color regions were carried out and the ID/IG values were calculated. The ID/IG ratios reduce with the decrease of deposited carbon content in Fig. 8(e), illustrating that the deposited carbon content and defects lessen, and the structure of GQDs may change during friction [44–46]. The results show that a dense and uneven friction film composed of highly disordered defective carbon was formed between sliding interfaces.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analyses of worn debris by SEM, EDS, and TEM characterizations
ce:section.ce:para: To further explore the mechanism of reducing wear of GQDs, the worn debris of GQDs-0.04 was collected and cleaned for SEM, EDS and TEM analyses. Fig. 9 shows the results that the worn debris is mainly composed of iron debris, and oxidation is produced in the friction process to form iron oxide, displaying a small cluster structure. Iron, carbon and oxygen elements of Fig. 9(b) are calculated in EDS mapping (Fig. 9(c)), a lot of C element is contained on worn debris. The C element is unevenly distributed on iron debris according to Fig. 9(d), GQDs participate in the friction process and are attached to the worn debris for hindering direct contact of friction pairs to achieve the effect of reducing wear rates. To further confirm the attachment of GQDs to iron oxide, worn debris was observed by TEM. Fig. 9(g) records the morphology of worn debris with a flocculent shape. The lattice structure of GQDs is observed under high resolution in Fig. 9(h) and Fig. 9(i), consistent with Fig. 2(a), indicating that some GQDs are attached to the worn debris in the process of producing worn debris, iron oxide with fluorescent GQDs attachment is formed. According to EDS analyses and SEM analyses, speculating that the iron debris continued to participate in the friction reaction after peeling off from the friction pairs, reducing the direct contact of the friction pairs to reduce wear.

In conclusion, a reasonable mechanism is proposed. Fig. 10 describes the mechanism and structures of the sliding friction machine and the four-ball friction machine. In the process of two kinds of friction conditions, the friction pairs contact with each other by point-line contact and point-point contact respectively. The friction makes the metal surface escape electrons and after emitting low-energy electrons, a positive charge is formed at the contact bump between the friction pairs [47]. GQDs with hydroxyl negative group participate in the friction reaction and form the friction film with negative properties that tend to adhere to positively charged worn surfaces. Due to electrostatic adsorption, the defective carbon film with a continuous distribution is attached to the iron oxide and sliding interfaces to fill the pits and reduce the direct contact of the friction pairs at the same time, achieving the effect of reducing wear. The remaining GQDs in the lubricants are partially reduced after friction, which can be intuitively reflected by the diminution of the fluorescence intensity of lubricants. The decrease of fluorescence intensity caused by friction process reflects that changes in lubricants status can be monitored by fluorescence intensity to explore the service life.

GO was used as the precursor to prepare fluorescent GQDs by a simple hydrothermal reaction, and then GQDs were dispersed in PEG to modulate lubricants. The fluorescence, friction and wear after friction were investigated. The conclusions are as follows:
(a)
Fluorescent GQDs synthesized by a simple hydrothermal reaction have a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm. Raman analysis shows that its properties are similar to the precursor GO, resulting in their excellent polarity.
(b)
As a polar additive, GQDs can effectively reduce the wear of friction surfaces. When the additive concentration is only 0.04%, it achieves a well anti-wear effect and reduce the wear rate by 40.5%.
(c)
With the flow of lubricants in the friction process, GQDs enter the contact areas of friction pairs and participate in friction reaction with high efficiency, forming continuously distributed disordered carbon deposited film.
(d)
The luminous intensity of fluorescent GQDs hybrid PEG lubricants decreases after friction owing to reduction of GQDs and friction chemistry reactions. State and the service life of lubricants can be monitored through fluorescence intensity.

Xiaoqiang Fan and Minhao Zhu developed the idea for this work, Zihan Zeng and Wen Li performed all measurements and characterization, Yuting Li, Zhuang Zhao and Kaiyue Lin analyzed and discussed the results, Zihan Zeng prepared the manuscript, Xiaoqiang Fan and Wen Li reviewed and commented on the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support provided by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52075458 and U2141211), and Analytical and Testing Center of Southwest Jiaotong University for providing testing services.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Smooth and dense graphene quantum dots-based lubricating coatings prepared by electrophoretic deposition
